examples.title: System Call for Process Creation
examples.description: Demonstrates how the fork() system call creates a new process
examples.code:
BEGIN
 // User program requests new process via system call
 FUNCTION createChildProcess()
 pid := TRAP(SYS_FORK) // Invoke system call
 
 IF pid == 0 THEN
 // This is the child process
 PRINT "Child process created with PID: ", GETPID()
 PRINT "Parent PID: ", GETPPID()
 EXECUTE childCode()
 ELSE IF pid > 0 THEN
 // This is the parent process
 PRINT "Parent: Child created with PID:", pid
 WAIT_FOR_CHILD(pid) // Wait for child to complete
 ELSE
 PRINT "Error: Fork failed"
 END IF
 END FUNCTION
END
examples.explanation: The fork() system call creates a new process by duplicating the calling process. It returns twice - once in parent (with child's PID) and once in child (with 0). The TRAP instruction switches to kernel mode.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for process control block allocation

examples.title: Memory Allocation System Call
examples.description: Shows memory allocation using brk/sbrk system call
examples.code:
BEGIN
 // Allocate heap memory via system call
 FUNCTION allocateMemory(size)
 currentBreak := TRAP(SYS_BRK, 0) // Get current break
 newBreak := currentBreak + size
 
 result := TRAP(SYS_BRK, newBreak)
 
 IF result == 0 THEN
 RETURN currentBreak // Return pointer to allocated memory
 ELSE
 RETURN -1 // Allocation failed
 END IF
 END FUNCTION
 
 // Example usage
 ptr := allocateMemory(1024)
 IF ptr != -1 THEN
 PRINT "Memory allocated successfully at:", ptr
 END IF
END
examples.explanation: The brk/sbrk system calls adjust the data segment size to allocate or deallocate heap memory. This demonstrates how user programs request memory from the OS kernel.

examples.title: File Open System Call
examples.description: Demonstrates file operations through system calls
examples.code:
BEGIN
 // Open file using open() system call
 FUNCTION openFile(filename, flags, permissions)
 // flags: O_RDONLY=0, O_WRONLY=1, O_RDWR=2
 // Can be combined with O_CREAT, O_APPEND, etc.
 
 fd := TRAP(SYS_OPEN, filename, flags, permissions)
 
 IF fd >= 0 THEN
 PRINT "File opened successfully, descriptor:", fd
 RETURN fd
 ELSE
 PRINT "Error opening file, code:", fd
 RETURN -1
 END IF
 END FUNCTION
 
 // Read from file
 FUNCTION readFile(fd, buffer, count)
 bytesRead := TRAP(SYS_READ, fd, buffer, count)
 RETURN bytesRead
 END FUNCTION
 
 // Write to file 
 FUNCTION writeFile(fd, data, length)
 bytesWritten := TRAP(SYS_WRITE, fd, data, length)
 RETURN bytesWritten
 END FUNCTION
 
 // Close file
 FUNCTION closeFile(fd)
 RETURN TRAP(SYS_CLOSE, fd)
 END FUNCTION
END
examples.explanation: File operations use system calls (open, read, write, close) that transition to kernel mode. Each process has a file descriptor table mapping integers to file objects managed by the OS.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for open/close, O(n) for read/write where n is data size
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) for file descriptors, O(n) for I/O buffers
